title: Explore DPM with the up metric

blocks.content: Use this guide to estimate data points per minute (DPM) for a familiar Prometheus metric. DPM measures how often each active series sends data to Grafana Cloud, so unexpectedly high DPM can increase ingestion volume and cost.

blocks.title: Open Explore with the up DPM query

blocks.content:
In this section, you will open Explore with an instant Prometheus query that counts samples for the `up` metric over the last minute. The query is shown here so you can copy it manually if your Explore state is different:

```promql
sort_desc(count_over_time({__name__="up"}[1m]))
```

The `up` metric is created by scrape-based Prometheus collection. If your metrics are pushed through OTLP instead of scraped, you might not have `up`; use another scraped metric name for this check.
Open **Explore** with the DPM query preloaded.

blocks.reftarget: div[data-testid='data-testid Data source picker select container']
blocks.content: Use the Explore data source picker to choose a Prometheus data source. If a Prometheus data source is already selected, keep it.
Keep the query type set to **Instant**, not **Range** or **Both**. Instant queries return a single value per series for the selected time.
You have run an instant query with a 1-minute lookback. A value near `1` means about one sample per minute. A value near `4` means about one sample every 15 seconds.

In this section, you will adapt the instant query for other metrics and learn when to avoid broad selectors. Use an exact metric name when you know what you want to inspect:

```promql
sort_desc(count_over_time({__name__="your_metric_name"}[1m]))
```

You can also use a regex matcher to inspect a family of metric names. This can be useful during exploration, but it can be expensive because Grafana may need to scan many metric names and series. Start exact, keep the `[1m]` lookback small, and broaden only when the result set is manageable.

Try this instant regex example in the local test environment:

```promql
sort_desc(count_over_time({__name__=~"up|prometheus_build_info"}[1m]))
```
Load the instant regex DPM query in Explore.
Run the regex query and compare the returned series.
You have learned how to adapt the DPM query, how to keep it instant, and when regex selectors can add query cost.

More to explore:

- [Reduce metrics costs by adjusting DPM](https://grafana.com/docs/grafana-cloud/cost-management-and-billing/analyze-costs/reduce-costs/metrics-costs/adjust-data-points-per-minute/)
- [Data points per minute in Grafana Cloud](https://grafana.com/blog/data-points-per-minute-in-grafana-cloud-what-you-need-to-know-about-dpm/)
- [dpm-finder](https://github.com/grafana-ps/dpm-finder) for larger metric scans through the Prometheus HTTP API
